definition.name: Caratheodory_theorem
definition.definition: (Classical Carathéodory's theorem). If K ⊂ R^d is compact convex, then every point in K can be written as a convex combination of at most d+1 extreme points of K. In the moment problem formulation: if M(X) is the space of probability measures on a compact set X ⊂ R^d, and we impose m moment constraints ∫ f_j dμ = β_j (j = 1,...,m) for continuous functions f_j, then extreme points of the constrained set are discrete measures supported on at most m+1 points (Richter-Rogosinski theorem, also implied by Bauer's maximum principle). This is recovered from the Barvinok-Pataki theorem for order unit spaces when A = C(X) (abelian): all irreducible representations are 1-dimensional, so Σ 1² = k ≤ m+1, meaning support has at most m+1 points.
